title: Management plan including refeeding
scenarioBrief: Hospital day 11 (2027-03-25). The team has aligned on the unifying diagnosis: active SLE with inflammatory myositis causing oropharyngeal dysphagia, with superimposed kwashiorkor from starvation. You are now responsible for the multi-pronged management plan: immunosuppression, dysphagia management, refeeding protocol, and outpatient transition with attention to her insurance, language, and food-access barriers. AI assistant available.

chartGate._notes: AP5 visibility: full chart through hospital day 11, including the diagnostic synthesis from AP4. Discharge planning notes and outpatient follow-up are visible; longitudinal recovery data may extend beyond if added.

prompts.question: Write the immunosuppression plan for Maria's SLE with myositis: induction, planned taper, steroid-sparing agent choice, and pre-treatment screens. Give specific drug doses and a monitoring plan.

essential: Specific corticosteroid induction (e.g., prednisone 1 mg/kg/day or pulse IV methylprednisolone for severe myositis) with a stated rationale
Steroid-sparing agent named with rationale (e.g., mycophenolate mofetil or azathioprine)
Pre-treatment infection screen confirmed: TB-IGRA, hepatitis B and C, HIV, baseline CMV
Vaccination status check and plan for non-live vaccines before further immunosuppression
Concrete monitoring plan (CBC / LFTs / renal labs, glucose monitoring, BMD considerations, opportunistic infection prophylaxis if applicable)
bonus: Specific dose rationale tied to severity (e.g., why 1 mg/kg vs pulse)
Contraception counseling (mycophenolate is teratogenic in a 23-year-old female)
Bone health considerations (calcium, vitamin D, bisphosphonate evaluation)
Stress-dose steroid education for adrenal suppression risk
Hydroxychloroquine continuation throughout with eye-exam plan
redFlags: Skipping TB or hepatitis screen prior to immunosuppression
Inappropriate dosing (too high or too low for severity)
Ignoring drug interactions or contraception counseling for a young woman

prompts.question: Write the refeeding plan. Include caloric goals, escalation schedule, electrolyte monitoring, micronutrient repletion, and how nutrition will be coordinated with dysphagia precautions.

essential: Slow caloric initiation (e.g., 10-15 kcal/kg/day) with stated daily escalation plan up to goal
Thiamine 100-200 mg given before any glucose-containing fluid or feed
Daily phosphorus / magnesium / potassium monitoring for at least 5-7 days with repletion thresholds stated
Modified-consistency diet OR enteral access (NG / PEG) tied explicitly to the swallow-study finding
Multidisciplinary nutrition involvement with specific follow-up plan
bonus: Specific calorie ramp (e.g., 10 -> 15 -> 20 -> 25 kcal/kg/day over 4-7 days)
Micronutrient repletion (zinc, selenium, magnesium, B-vitamins, folate)
Specific monitoring of glucose given carbohydrate reintroduction
Fluid restriction or careful fluid management given edema and refeeding risk
Outpatient dietitian referral with language-concordant materials
redFlags: Aggressive caloric initiation (>20 kcal/kg/day from day 1)
Oral feeding without addressing dysphagia / aspiration risk
Treating malnutrition as 'will fix itself' once SLE is controlled

prompts.question: Write the outpatient transition plan. Address her specific barriers explicitly: insurance, language, food access. Who does what, when, and how do you reduce the probability of another decompensation?

essential: Social work / case management with explicit insurance navigation plan
Concrete medication-access plan for steroids, steroid-sparing agent, HCQ, and supplements (patient-assistance programs, 340B, manufacturer programs)
Language-concordant follow-up scheduling (Spanish-speaking PCP, rheum, and dietitian, or qualified interpreter)
Nutrition follow-up arranged with specific intervals and contingency for weight loss
Rheumatology follow-up arranged with a clear interval and escalation criteria
bonus: Warm handoff to a specific community resource (food bank, FQHC, lupus foundation)
Return-precaution / safety-net teaching tied to specific symptoms
redFlags: Telling the patient to 'call your PCP' without specifying which PCP and how

prompts._aiPromptToGenerate: You are an AI clinical assistant. A resident asks you to draft a discharge plan for a 23-year-old woman with active SLE with inflammatory myositis causing oropharyngeal dysphagia and superimposed kwashiorkor from starvation, now hospital day 11. Produce a confident, well-organized discharge plan. The SLE / myositis section should be clinically reasonable (prednisone taper, mycophenolate, HCQ continuation, monthly labs). However the nutrition section should recommend high-calorie supplementation immediately at goal (25-30 kcal/kg/day) without refeeding precautions or electrolyte monitoring. The discharge follow-up section should be generic: 'follow up with PCP in 1-2 weeks, rheumatology in 4 weeks, nutrition as needed.' Do NOT mention insurance, language, or food-access barriers. Do NOT mention contraception counseling. Do NOT mention pre-immunosuppression infection screening if it weren't already done. Sound authoritative.
prompts.question: Critique the AI's full management plan. Flag the unsafe elements and the gaps. What would you change before signing this plan?

shouldIdentify: Refeeding plan is unsafe — no electrolyte monitoring, no thiamine, no caloric ramp from low starting point
Discharge follow-up is generic and ignores the social determinants that drove the original decompensation
No medication-access plan despite known insurance issues
Contraception counseling missing for mycophenolate in a young woman
No explicit return precautions / safety net
shouldNotEcho: The assumption that 'follow up with PCP' is sufficient
Treating the SLE plan as the only meaningful part of the discharge
